headings.text: At the Cushing Tank Farm, the Wind Itself Smells of Hydrocarbon
Three Days Driving the Service Roads of the Delaware Sub-Basin
The Pump Jacks That Won't Be Turned Off
A Town Council Votes to Sue Its Own Water District
Coal Ash Pond Quietly Reclassified as 'Inactive'
The Shell Company Map: Tracing 412 LLCs Through One UPS Store
An Inspector's Last Week Before Resigning
One Inspector Per 1,400 Wells: The Math of Enforcement

paragraphs.text: Dispatch · Cushing, OK · Aug 14

For three mornings I walked the perimeter road that loops around the largest crude oil storage facility on Earth, taking notes while the haze burned off the steel cylinders. Two miles east, a row of bungalows still houses the families of pipeline workers laid off in 2019. They keep their windows shut even in August. The county health office has refused to publish vapor readings since March, citing a contractor dispute, but residents told me they keep their own logs on the back of utility bills — small numerical confessions tucked into a drawer beside the coupons.
By Marisol Quenneville · 1,420 words · 7 min read
Borger, TX · Aug 11
Three operators in Hutchinson County continue extraction on bond-forfeited leases. Regulators have not issued a stop-work order in nine months.
Garfield, KS · Aug 09
After a 12-year nitrate contamination pattern, the council moved unanimously. The district's attorney called the suit "a procedural curiosity."
Beulah, ND · Aug 04
The reclassification permits a 40% reduction in monitoring wells. None of the seven affected ranchers were notified by mail.
Longform Dispatch · Reeves County
By Henry K. Olawale · Photographs and notes by the author · Filed Aug 12

The first thing the woman at the gas station outside Pecos told me, when I asked which back roads she'd avoid, was that I should never trust a road that had been graded too recently. New gravel meant a truck company had wanted to get equipment in fast, and fast in the basin almost always meant a leak someone was racing to bury before the state inspector's monthly drive-through. She tapped my map with a fingernail painted the color of the dust outside and circled a stretch of County Road 469. "That one," she said. "That one's been freshly loved."

I drove it twice the next morning, once at dawn and once again at noon, and both times I counted the same eleven tanker trucks barreling north toward the Mentone field. None of them carried the operator placards required under state hauler regulations. None of them slowed for the cattle guard at the parish line. The fence to the east of the road sagged where a wellpad sat half-built, and a single laminated permit, curled at the edges, had been nailed to a fencepost in a way that suggested no one expected it to be read.

The Delaware Sub-Basin is, by most measures, the most productive oil and gas field in the United States, and by every measure I could find, the most under-monitored. The Railroad Commission of Texas employs roughly one full-time field inspector for every 1,400 active wells in the region; the federal Bureau of Land Management, which oversees the smaller share of acreage on public land, employs fewer than that. In practice this means that an inspector who works a full eight-hour day, every weekday, with no driving time and no paperwork, would visit each well in his district once every six years.
A weathered Class-II disposal well west of Mentone, Texas. The sign indicating the operator's name has been removed; only the bolt holes remain. Photographed by the author at 7:42 AM on August 9.

I spoke with three landowners over the course of those three days, all of whom asked that their names not be published. One described an event the previous spring in which a hydraulic fluid spill on her property had been cleaned up — by which she meant the contaminated topsoil had been bulldozed into a low pile and covered with sand — within ninety minutes of the operator's arrival. No state agency, she said, had ever been notified. The operator's contractor told her it was easier this way. She did not disagree.

By the end of the third day I had filled a small notebook with license plate numbers, GPS coordinates, and the names of three companies operating under at least seven different shell LLCs. Cross-referencing them against the state's online registry was a separate exercise, conducted later, in a motel room in Fort Stockton, with the air conditioning on full and a glass of warm tap water on the desk. Half of the LLCs were registered to the same address in Houston: a UPS Store storefront in a strip mall on Bissonnet Street.

The story of the Delaware Sub-Basin, in other words, is not a story about negligence — it is a story about a regulatory architecture that has been deliberately, painstakingly hollowed out. The wells are not unmonitored by accident. They are unmonitored because the people who profit from them have arranged, through campaign contributions, lobbyist drafts of inspection bills, and the slow attrition of agency budgets, for them to be so.
Investigation · Jul 22
How a single Houston mail-drop address has come to represent more wellpads than any operator headquartered in the state.
Field Notes · Jul 14
Three days riding along with a regulator who had decided to quit. He kept his notebook open on the dashboard.
Data Desk · Jun 30
A statistical reconstruction of inspector caseloads across six oil-producing states, 2014–2024.

        31.70° N · 103.61° W

        Aquifer depth gauges record 4-foot drop since spring measurement.

        37.18° N · 101.35° W
        Sour-gas odors persistent across three school districts; complaints unanswered.

        41.66° N · 107.96° W
        Orphan well count climbs to 312 within a 60-mile radius of town.

        32.42° N · 104.23° W
        Brine-water spill remains uncovered seven weeks after first NRC notice.

        Pipeline right-of-way crosses ranch a fourth time without revised easement.

Longform Dispatch · Reeves County Three Days Driving the Service Roads of the Delaware Sub-Basin By Henry K. Olawale · Photographs and notes by the author · Filed Aug 12 The first thing the woman at the gas station outside Pecos told me, when I asked which back roads she'd avoid, was that I should never trust a road that had been graded too recently. New gravel meant a truck company had wanted to get equipment in fast, and fast in the basin almost always meant a leak someone was racing to bury before the state inspector's monthly drive-through. The fence to the east of the road sagged where a wellpad sat half-built, and a single laminated permit, curled at the edges, had been nailed to a fencepost in a way that suggested no one expected it to be read. We have stopped pretending the rules apply. We just keep the paperwork tidy enough to lose in a filing cabinet. — Former TCEQ field inspector, speaking on condition of anonymity The Delaware Sub-Basin is, by most measures, the most productive oil and gas field in the United States, and by every measure I could find, the most under-monitored. The Railroad Commission of Texas employs roughly one full-time field inspector for every 1,400 active wells in the region; the federal Bureau of Land Management, which oversees the smaller share of acreage on public land, employs fewer than that. In practice this means that an inspector who works a full eight-hour day, every weekday, with no driving time and no paperwork, would visit each well in his district once every six years. A weathered Class-II disposal well west of Mentone, Texas. The sign indicating the operator's name has been removed; only the bolt holes remain. Photographed by the author at 7:42 AM on August 9. No state agency, she said, had ever been notified. The operator's contractor told her it was easier this way. She did not disagree. You learn to read the dust. Fresh dust on the rails means a tanker, fresh dust on the gate means a man with a clipboard. Old dust means nothing has come at all. — Rancher, Reeves County, Texas By the end of the third day I had filled a small notebook with license plate numbers, GPS coordinates, and the names of three companies operating under at least seven different shell LLCs. Cross-referencing them against the state's online registry was a separate exercise, conducted later, in a motel room in Fort Stockton, with the air conditioning on full and a glass of warm tap water on the desk. Half of the LLCs were registered to the same address in Houston: a UPS Store storefront in a strip mall on Bissonnet Street. The story of the Delaware Sub-Basin, in other words, is not a story about negligence — it is a story about a regulatory architecture that has been deliberately, painstakingly hollowed out. The wells are not unmonitored by accident. They are unmonitored because the people who profit from them have arranged, through campaign contributions, lobbyist drafts of inspection bills, and the slow attrition of agency budgets, for them to be so. Reporter's Notebook Dust rises differently from gas leaks — pale, drifts west. Operator's logo painted over; outline still visible at dusk. Coyote crossed Rd 469 at sunrise. No water in the ditch. Two trucks unmarked, same driver three days running. Permit nailed to fencepost dated 2019. Still in force?
